title: Notebook-first medical ML is still serious research
excerpt: A notebook can be the most faithful record of a machine-learning study when it preserves data inspection, preprocessing, training, and evaluation in the same visible sequence.

links.label: Alzheimer MRI project
links.description: Project page for the MRI classification study.

links.label: Open in Colab
links.description: Notebook entry point in Google Colab.

pageSections.title: Polish is not the same thing as fidelity
pageSections.markdown:
There is a common habit in technical presentation to treat notebooks as provisional artifacts and packaged code as the only serious final form. In research work, that distinction can be misleading. A notebook often preserves the actual order in which the work happened: data loading, visual inspection, preprocessing, model definition, training, evaluation, and the first attempts at comparison.

That sequence matters. It keeps the experiment legible. When the artifact is rewritten too aggressively into a cleaner package, some of that visibility disappears, and with it the ability to understand how the result was produced.

pageSections.title: The data should remain visible throughout the study
pageSections.description: MRI examples from the notebook make the classification task concrete. They also make it easier to evaluate whether preprocessing and model choices remain proportionate to the structure of the underlying images.
pageSections.caption: MRI sample montage extracted from the project notebook.

pageSections.title: A notebook preserves the sequence of technical decisions
pageSections.markdown:
In the Alzheimer MRI study, the notebook shows where the data is loaded from, how the class structure is framed, how augmentation enters the workflow, and how a baseline convolutional model gives way to later comparison and ensemble logic. That is not incidental detail. It is the structure of the experiment itself.

When that sequence remains visible, the study becomes easier to evaluate. It is possible to ask whether the dataset split is sensible, whether the image sizing and preprocessing are appropriate, and whether later modeling choices genuinely extend the analysis rather than only making it look more sophisticated.

pageSections.title: Single-slice views keep the workflow grounded in actual inputs
pageSections.description: Close inspection of input images is part of the work, not decoration around it. It keeps the discussion of preprocessing and model behavior tied to the data that the model actually sees.
pageSections.caption: Single MRI slice extracted from notebook output.

items.label: Disease staging
items.context: The study distinguishes progressive stages rather than reducing the task to a binary split.

items.label: Execution environment
items.context: The project keeps experimentation, training, and evaluation in one visible file.

items.label: Initial model
items.context: A baseline convolutional model anchors later comparisons.

items.label: Evaluation extension
items.context: The notebook moves beyond a single model output into comparison.

pageSections.title: A faithful experiment log is often more valuable than a cleaner façade
pageSections.markdown:
In scientific and medical machine learning, clarity about method matters as much as presentational polish. A notebook that keeps assumptions, preprocessing, training, and evaluation visible can be more useful than a cleaner interface that hides the path from data to result.

That is why I think notebook-first work should be presented directly when it remains the most accurate representation of the study. The goal is not to make the artifact look less technical. It is to make the technical work easier to read honestly.
